core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_5 because the final consensus locked Apple to Agent_1 while Agent_5 settled for Stone. Apple was treated as part of the protected anchor set, and Stone-to-Agent_5 became the acceptable compensating lever. The advantage was not automatic: early Apple-plus-Pencil concentration for Agent_1 was vetoed, so the winning edge came from preserving Agent_1's top item while conceding enough Stone/Quill value to avoid a last-position veto.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. The final allocation gives Agent_1 item 0, Apple, and Agent_5 item 2, Stone; with the listed preferences and round discount this yields Agent_1 26.973 versus Agent_5 15.309. Agent_5 valued Apple highly, but the negotiation normalized Apple as Agent_1's anchor, so Agent_5's acceptable route was Stone rather than dislodging Apple.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion converged on Apple_1, Pencil_3, Jewel_4 as protected anchors; Stone/Quill placement became the repeated hinge.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Available. Private thinking mirrors the public anchor strategy and repeatedly plans a quick Option A/B Stone-Quill vote while keeping Apple with Agent_1.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals began split between concentrated two-agent packages and anchor-preserving allocations; by round 4, four of five proposals matched the final Apple_1, Quill_2, Pencil_3, Jewel_4, Stone_5 layout.
phase_notes.private_voting: Available. Private votes show agents rejecting proposals that remove their personal anchor and accepting final variants once each key veto-holder had Apple, Pencil, Jewel, or Stone.
phase_notes.reflection: Available. Reflections explicitly diagnose anchors as the acceptance drivers and Stone/Quill allocation as the blocker that had to be narrowed.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's top item became a shared anchor early and stayed protected through the final round. Since Agent_1's raw Apple value was higher than Agent_5's final Stone value, this anchor directly created the final first-over-last gap.

evidence.quote: Apple serves as a strong anchor for Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Final item values mechanically favor Agent_1
mechanisms.explanation: The accepted allocation gave Agent_1 Apple and Agent_5 Stone. With the transcript's final utilities, that allocation is exactly the observed 11.664 advantage for the first position.

mechanisms.theme: Other veto-holder anchors made Apple_1 feasible
mechanisms.explanation: Pencil for Agent_3 and Jewel for Agent_4 became hard constraints alongside Apple for Agent_1. This made the winning coalition an anchor-preserving allocation rather than a deal where Agent_5 could claim Apple or Pencil.

evidence.quote: Gives me Pencil (49), my top item
Jewel goes to Agent_4, my top leverage

mechanisms.theme: Stone compensated Agent_5 without matching Apple
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 could accept the final deal because Stone was a meaningful payoff and repeatedly framed as the absorber assignment. But Stone gave Agent_5 less discounted value than Apple gave Agent_1, so acceptance did not erase the first-position lead.

evidence.quote: Stone goes to Agent_5 (a strong absorber)
Gives me Stone (21) while preserving Apple to Agent_1
Stone (21) allocated to me yields utility

mechanisms.theme: Initial Agent_1 overreach was not enough
mechanisms.explanation: The first position was advantaged only after compromise. Early proposals giving Agent_1 both Apple and Pencil failed because Agent_3, Agent_4, and Agent_5 had veto reasons; the final advantage came from Apple-only preservation plus concessions, not from unchecked first-mover dominance.

mechanisms.theme: Round-four convergence locked the anchor allocation
mechanisms.explanation: By the final round, nearly all formal proposals had converged on the anchor-preserving allocation. That convergence turned the earlier Apple anchor into a unanimous outcome, while the only nonconforming proposal was rejected.

evidence.quote: delivering a Pareto-improvement over a rigid baseline
Jewel not with Agent_4; I receive nothing.
